solutions.solution: We cannot treat such rumours as involve the President's life, as baseless. Hence III does not follow and, instead, I and II follow.
If an extreme action, II and III are better because they are more likely to improve the situation than I.
Precautions must be taken for better chances of a crisis. Hence, I and II follow, but III is an extreme action.
The situation is quite serious and some action should be given detailed secondary treatment; hence, III does not follow.

It is obvious such health a labile of escaping from a problem that there is civil disobedience means that we are making it lighter because it is proper officials presently. III is also not proper. To stop child labour, we must stop and punish those who employ children. To punish the children themselves would be inhuman.

I and II are proper courses of action. Good things must be (i) promoted and (ii) prevented. III is not proper; we cannot ban a movie only because it is not similar to a particular movie. (On these grounds, even a Pakistani would be banned!!)

There is doubt that the government has immunity and they are biased action. III has a positive bias as it suggests unfair protection by the government. II has a negative bias as it suggests unfair control of the government. The best thing would be to let the mutual funds determine their own future. Again, here we have the idea of prevailing politics, privatisation and non-interference is the buzzword. Some years ago, we were advocating other licensing and control (as in II) or safeguarding from competition (as in III). But today we call these licencing raj protections, respectively.
I is an extreme and inhuman action. Both II and III are necessary steps towards the prevention of AIDS.

Both I and II are extreme and irrational actions. A good government should be willing to listen to criticism, on one hand, and should not abandon its plans just because of some criticism, on the other. III is a proper action and it gives the given an opportunity to put forward their pleas before a decision - so ruthless towards them - is implemented.

Both I and II are questionable. I is not acceptable; it is an extreme action and some efforts to redress the situation under the popularly elected government must precede it. II appears without any explanation; why should one take privatisation to be the cure of all evils? III is proper; the government concerned must be asked to re-evaluate their system and correct the faults.
I and II are obvious and do not need any explanation. III does not follow. It is ridiculous and, in fact, laughable. Pakistan may simply return the copies after getting them xeroxed.
